tips.detail: Stand aside from the marked lines on the platform and wait until all passengers have exited before stepping onto the train.

tips.tip: Have your transit code or card ready before the gate
tips.detail: Prepare your QR code or transit card while in line so you don't hold up others at the turnstile. Alipay and WeChat transit QR codes work in most cities.

tips.tip: Give up priority seats for those in need
tips.detail: Seats near the doors marked in a different color are reserved for elderly, pregnant, disabled passengers, and those with small children. Vacate them promptly when someone in need boards.

tips.tip: Do not pretend to sleep to avoid giving up your seat
tips.detail: Passengers commonly notice and social media in China frequently criticizes this behavior. Offer your seat gracefully when an elderly or pregnant person is standing nearby.

tips.tip: Keep luggage compact and out of the aisle
tips.detail: Place bags between your feet or on your lap. During peak hours, large suitcases should be kept in the luggage area near the train doors and not block pathways.

tips.tip: Avoid eating strong-smelling food on transit
tips.detail: Eating on the metro is officially banned in many Chinese cities. Even on long-distance trains, avoid foods with strong odors like instant noodles in enclosed carriages to be considerate.

tips.tip: Keep phone calls quiet or text instead
tips.detail: Use earphones for calls and speak softly. On high-speed trains, step to the vestibule area between carriages for longer conversations.

tips.tip: Do not play media on loudspeaker
tips.detail: Playing videos, music, or games without earphones is considered very rude on Chinese public transport. Many metro systems now explicitly broadcast reminders against this.

tips.tip: Respect queues at bus stops and ticket counters
tips.detail: While queue culture varies by city, cutting in line is universally frowned upon. Follow the crowd order and use designated lines where marked.

tips.tip: Do not push or shove when boarding
tips.detail: During rush hour in cities like Beijing and Shanghai, crowding is intense. Be patient and avoid aggressive pushing—wait for the next train if this one is full.

tips.tip: Confirm the meter is running when you enter a taxi
tips.detail: Always ensure the driver starts the meter. At airports and train stations, prefer the official taxi queue over touts who approach you in the arrivals hall.

tips.tip: Do not be alarmed by frequent honking
tips.detail: Drivers in China often honk as a courtesy warning rather than out of anger. It usually means 'I'm here' or 'coming through.' Stay calm and stay alert when crossing streets.

tips.tip: Always wear a seatbelt in the back seat of taxis
tips.detail: Chinese law requires all passengers to wear seatbelts. Rear-seat seatbelts are sometimes tucked into the seat—pull them out and buckle up before departure.

tips.tip: Share your ride details and verify the car before getting in
tips.detail: When using DiDi, confirm the license plate, car model, and driver photo in the app matches the vehicle. Use the in-app share feature to send trip details to a trusted contact.

tips.tip: Set your destination in Chinese for better navigation
tips.detail: Most DiDi drivers read Chinese only. Type your destination in Chinese characters or drop a pin on the map. Keep the driver's chat window open in case they message you for pickup clarification.
